Summary: This study compares the diagnostic performance of machine learning (ML) classifiers based on unenhanced CT radiomics with that of a radiologist in diagnosing cystic renal masses (CRMs). The study found that ML classifiers, particularly KNN, showed comparable diagnostic accuracy to the radiologist and could potentially be used for screening CRMs.

Condition1.reason: The study mentions that total tumor segmentation was performed by two radiologists using 3D Slicer.

Condition2.reason: The study mentions that segmentation was performed by radiologists, indicating that it was not fully automated.

Condition3.reason: The study extracts radiomic features using the PyRadiomics package, which is a form of hand-crafted feature extraction.

Condition4.reason: The study uses extracted radiomic features in a tabulated format for machine learning modeling.

Condition5.reason: The study does not use end-to-end deep learning. Instead, it relies on hand-crafted feature extraction followed by traditional machine learning classifiers.

Item1.reason: The study mentions using the CLEAR checklist and the radiomics quality score (RQS) to evaluate the quality of research.

Item2.reason: The study explicitly defines inclusion and exclusion criteria for patient enrollment.

Item3.reason: The reference standard is histopathological results, which is a high-quality reference standard.

Item4.reason: The study involves data from two hospitals (Guangzhou and Zhuhai).

Item5.reason: The imaging data source is unenhanced CT, which is a clinically relevant imaging technique for evaluating CRMs.

Item6.reason: The study reports the image acquisition parameters, including tube voltage, tube current, section interval, section thickness, and matrix size.

Item7.reason: The study does not explicitly mention the time interval between imaging and the reference standard (histopathological results).

Item8.reason: The study describes the segmentation methodology, mentioning that masses were delineated on the original CT images using 3D Slicer and that radiologists were allowed to observe the enhanced CT images to outline the shape and edges of the masses more accurately. It also mentions that the entire CRM was carefully and manually segmented to avoid beyond-boundary or insufficient filling. Segmentation of whole masses was performed by associate chief radiologists (T.L. and L.H.) with more than 15 years of experience in abdominal radiography. The radiologists were allowed to observe the enhanced CT images to outline the shape and edges of the masses more accurately, which is a transparent description of the segmentation methodology. The study also mentions that the radiologists were allowed to observe the enhanced CT images to delineate the boundaries of the masses, which may have led to bias in practical applications. This is a limitation of the study, but it does not negate the fact that the segmentation methodology was described.

Item9.reason: The segmentation was performed manually by radiologists, not using a fully automated method.

Item10.reason: The segmentation was performed by two radiologists, not a single reader or automated tool.

Item11.reason: The study mentions using 3D Slicer for mass segmentation and calculation of radiomic features. It also mentions that the extracted features were classified into seven categories and that 14 filters were applied to the original images. The study also mentions that the images were analyzed for each patient. This is an appropriate use of image preprocessing techniques with transparent description.

Item12.reason: The study used PyRadiomics for feature extraction.

Item13.reason: The study describes the feature types (first-order, two-dimensional, etc.) and mentions the use of wavelet filters. It also provides information on the software and version used (3D Slicer version 5.2.1 and PyRadiomics package). The study also mentions that the extracted features were classified into seven categories and that 14 filters were applied to the original images. The study also mentions that the images were analyzed for each patient. This is a transparent reporting of feature extraction parameters.

Item14.reason: The study used intraclass correlation coefficients (ICCs) to evaluate consistency across the radiomic features; features with ICCs >0.75 were considered stable and were included in this analysis.

Item15.reason: The study used the least absolute shrinkage and selection operator (LASSO) method to select the most suitable radiomic features to develop a radiomic signature with the “glmnet” package. First, 10-fold cross-validation was performed to obtain the optimal parameter λ14 by 1,000 iterations. Second, the LASSO method based on the optimal parameter λ was used to calculate the coefficient of each feature, and features with non-zero coefficients were selected.14 Finally, bidirectional elimination was used to further filter the radiomic features selected using the LASSO method;15 the “mass” package in the R software (version 4.2.2) was used for bidirectional elimination (Figure 1).

Item16.reason: The study uses LASSO and bidirectional elimination to reduce dimensionality, which is appropriate given the data size.

Item17.reason: The study does not use end-to-end deep learning.

Item18.reason: The study mentions that the cases from Center 1 were allocated to the training set, while the cases from Center 2 were assigned to the validation set for external validation. The study also mentions that the data were divided into 10 parts; nine parts were used for training in turn, and the remaining part was used to estimate the efficacy of the models. During the process of fine-tuning the models, the grid search method was employed to select the best combination of hyperparameter values. This is a proper data partitioning process.

Item19.reason: The study acknowledges the potential for confounding factors due to the use of different scanners and mentions that it may be necessary to apply a data harmonization procedure. The study also mentions that the composition of the validation set was not balanced (15 benign and 30 malignant CRMs), which may have led to potential risks and affected the validation results. This is a handling of confounding factors.

Item20.reason: The study reports appropriate accuracy metrics such as AUC, accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, PPV, and NPV. It also includes confidence intervals.

Item21.reason: The study reports 95% confidence intervals (CIs) for the performance metrics.

Item22.reason: The study includes calibration curves for the ML classifiers in both the training and validation sets.

Item23.reason: The study focuses on unenhanced CT images but does not explicitly compare its performance to multi-parametric imaging or justify the use of a single imaging set.

Item24.reason: The study compares the performance of the ML classifiers with that of manual diagnosis by a radiologist.

Item25.reason: The study does not compare the ML models with a simple baseline reference model.
